april 4 2022. starting with markets we
have stocks up a little bit this morning
commodities up also a little bit crypto
down a little bit dollar up global bond
yields mixed uh big movers this morning
it's not a ton of action to report a lot
of stuff trading sideways this morning
but uh what i thought was noteworthy was
the european bond yields diverging from
u.s rates that's pretty noteworthy uh

the uk and germany
down seven basis points day over day
whereas you got 10-year treasury yield
30-year treasury yields up you know one
to three basis points respectively uh
big news this morning at russian and
ukrainian uh negotiators are set to
resume uh peace talks on monday and this
is after the european union uh joined
the us and condemning russia for its war
crimes but in several ukrainian towns
it's saying that the bloc will act as a
matter of urgency quote to work on
additional sanctions against moscow
we'll see if they have any real teeth
the fed minutes later this week will get
those on wednesday ecb minutes on
thursday we'll get some clues as to the
pace and timing of interest rate hikes
mary daley all but more or less
guarantee we're going to get a 50 basis
point hike in may uh barring some major
catastrophe along the way but we're also
looking for key details on quantitative
tightening as well in those minutes and
obviously in the may fomc meeting uh on
the macro front uh while friday's mars
jobs report certainly garnered all the
headlines uh but the the the ism
manufacturing pmi data actually provided
investors with more of a signal as it
relates to the outlook for the us and by
extension the global economies unpacking
the data
you had headline ism slow to 57.1 that's
the lowest point we've seen since
september 2020 new order slowed to 53.8
that's the lowest point we've seen since
may of 2020 uh employment which is
obviously a lagging indicator that
ticked up uh to 56.3 that's the highest
price we've seen since march of 2021.
prices continue to accelerate 87.1
that's the highest price we've seen
since june of 21. so some real impulse
there and that's what's underpinning our
view that the fed will be incrementally
more hawkish the next time we hear from
them from fomc perspective and then
something we called out going back to
november uh percentage of um supply
responders reporting supplier to slower
supplier deliveries that tick down uh to
34.8 that's the lowest we've seen since
january so amelia rating supply chain
disruptions but maybe not the good kind
of amelia region maybe the bad kind in
terms of demand destruction um and so
taking it to the chart this morning
what i'm showing here is ism uh new
orders up here in the blue line the red
line uh sorry
yep i assume up here in the blue line
the red line is the ism inventories and
what i'm showing in the bottom panel
here is the spread and what you notice
taking this data all the way back as far
as we can get it to 1948
every time you notice there's a negative
print with a few exceptions in kind of
mid 1950s or early 1950s in a mid-1950s
uh mid-1960s and then sort of um kind of
you know 2011 2012.
there haven't been a lot of exceptions
of negative prints that didn't
immediately lead into recession now
again i think they were a little bit
ways away from getting to recession in
the u.s economy i still believe that's a
kind of a 1h23 event in terms of our
ability to assess that from an economic
standpoint but certainly this negative
1.7 number which is the lowest print
we've seen since may of 2020 should
raise some alarm bells incrementally as
it relates to what the yield curve has
been signaling to investors uh for a few
months now so definitely be aware of
this this is one of those stealth
leading indicators from a business cycle
perspective that allows you to make some
of these big macro pivots from an asset
allocation standpoint and hopefully
you're appreciating that we're
delivering to you for free in this
morning uh on the macro minute with

in its probable range
